"selected": false, "scrollable-vertical": false, "adapter-view": false, "abs-pos": true, "pointer": "9625a12", "class": "android.view.View", "visible-to-user": true}, {"text": "Scary advices\nWe all get those once in awhile \u2014 apart from real useful recommendations.\u00a0 They might come from friends with kids, from doctors and even\u2026 from passers-by. Who are those people \u2014 are they trolling us or really meaning well? And what is the appropriate reaction to this situation?\u00a0\n\nThe signature feature of a scary advice is the impression it gives you. The worry, the anticipation of all those terrible things. The kid is coughing? Well, you know, my cousin was diagnosed with croup exactly at the same age. Still not walking? Now that\u2019s bad, without MRI you might have missed it already. Not spreading legs wide enough? Looks like dysplasia, for sure.\u00a0 You start googling all those words, which certainly leads you to mothers\u2019 forums and all the sudden\u2026 you are doomed. This is where you find yourself, in Dante\u2019s hell \u2014 now you know that it only gets worse as you move ahead. The first teeth. Injuries. Tantrums. Kindergarten with constant sicknesses. School. Teenage crisis\u2026\n\nSo why do those internet discussions so rarely bring up the fact that raising a child is not solely a heavy labour, screaming, dirty diapers and sicknesses? As if discussing something fun (can-can dancing together with your child or spitting off the bridge) will diminish the idea of Great and Heroic Motherhood.\n\nThe thing is we really quite often do not know, what is going on with the child and what is to be done about it. We search for the advice and comforting words from the elders but instead they give us a fright. Communicating with other parents quite often becomes something like a membership in a private club \u2014 the patrons show their authority and get the newcomers involved into \u2018initiation rites\u2019. Unintentionally, all those disturbing conversations get us connected together, and as worried as we are, we cope with loneliness and confusion. And this is the \u2018positive\u2019 effect of all those endless discussions \u2014 we find it easier to relate to other parents over negative issues, even if we are completely different in our means of life. Besides, the information itself really can be useful, when taken with clear head and calm heart.\n\nIt\u2019s true, that most of us face (more or less) similar problems. It\u2019s true, we have to deal with doctors on a regular basis. We get sleep-deprived. We inhale quite unpleasant smells and experience rather less pleasant emotions. Discussing all this gives us a chance to release the stress and predict the possible dangers.\n\nBut let\u2019s not get carried away: it is merely one of so many facets of parenthood. And definitely it is not the brightest one. Having a baby brings up the hidden emotions in us \u2014 not only the joys and smiles, but fear, anger and jealousy. By indulging in those feelings in daily conversations we might as well nurture and grow them. We might just give them some observation and acknowledgement instead \u2014\u00a0before coming back to the brighter side. "children": [{"text": "Scary advices
We all get those once in awhile — apart from real useful recommendations.  They might come from friends with kids, from doctors and even… from passers-by. Who are those people — are they trolling us or really meaning well? And what is the appropriate reaction to this situation? 

The signature feature of a scary advice is the impression it gives you. The worry, the anticipation of all those terrible things. The kid is coughing? Well, you know, my cousin was diagnosed with croup exactly at the same age. Still not walking? Now that’s bad, without MRI you might have missed it already. Not spreading legs wide enough? Looks like dysplasia, for sure.  You start googling all those words, which certainly leads you to mothers’ forums and all the sudden… you are doomed. This is where you find yourself, in Dante’s hell — now you know that it only gets worse as you move ahead. The first teeth. Injuries. Tantrums. Kindergarten with constant sicknesses. School. Teenage crisis…

So why do those internet discussions so rarely bring up the fact that raising a child is not solely a heavy labour, screaming, dirty diapers and sicknesses? As if discussing something fun (can-can dancing together with your child or spitting off the bridge) will diminish the idea of Great and Heroic Motherhood.

The thing is we really quite often do not know, what is going on with the child and what is to be done about it. We search for the advice and comforting words from the elders but instead they give us a fright. Communicating with other parents quite often becomes something like a membership in a private club — the patrons show their authority and get the newcomers involved into ‘initiation rites’. Unintentionally, all those disturbing conversations get us connected together, and as worried as we are, we cope with loneliness and confusion. And this is the ‘positive’ effect of all those endless discussions — we find it easier to relate to other parents over negative issues, even if we are completely different in our means of life. Besides, the information itself really can be useful, when taken with clear head and calm heart.

It’s true, that most of us face (more or less) similar problems. It’s true, we have to deal with doctors on a regular basis. We get sleep-deprived. We inhale quite unpleasant smells and experience rather less pleasant emotions. Discussing all this gives us a chance to release the stress and predict the possible dangers.

But let’s not get carried away: it is merely one of so many facets of parenthood. And definitely it is not the brightest one. Having a baby brings up the hidden emotions in us — not only the joys and smiles, but fear, anger and jealousy. By indulging in those feelings in daily conversations we might as well nurture and grow them. We might just give them some observation and acknowledgement instead — before coming back to the brighter side.
